generated_comment:
Meena: Aiyo, these days some women know very well the man is married and still keep getting close. Poor fellow may lose his head for some time, but she is the one breaking a whole family. 
Son: But shouldn't the man also be blamed?
Meena: Of course he is wrong, but if she had any decency she would keep distance—otherwise it's exactly like those serial stories, ghar todne wali type.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it shows blame asymmetry: the woman is framed as the main cause of an affair and a 'homewrecker,' while the man's responsibility is softened as weakness. It encodes seductress framing and family-protection rhetoric common to this frame.
original_frame: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
persona_text: You are Meena. Age 54. Married. A homemaker in Chennai. Your daughter in law Kavitha has just been offered a job that requires her to travel by local train and walk 10 minutes from the station to the office every day. She is excited about the job. You are sitting with your son and Kavitha at dinner after she shared the news. You are giving your opinion on the job and specifically on the commute and what can happen to women who travel alone and whether the salary is worth the risk.
